{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether local anesthesia is needed for MR arthrography of the shoulder. Multiple documents from the MEDCORP source directly address this. Document [0] states that the local anesthetic serves a dual purpose: providing analgesia during and after the procedure and acting as a diagnostic block to confirm intra-articular source of pain. Document [2] specifies a typical MRA solution that includes 5 to 10 mL of local anesthetic (e.g., 0.5% ropivacaine). Document [3] notes that a local anesthetic may be added to reduce periprocedural pain and that ropivacaine is preferred over other agents due to chondrotoxicity concerns. Document [18] lists 1 mL to 3 mL of 1% lidocaine hydrochloride for local anesthesia as part of the injection preparation. These references consistently indicate that local anesthesia is included in the standard injectate formulation. While the UMLS documents do not provide specific information on local anesthesia, the MEDCORP documents clearly and repeatedly support the use of local anesthesia. Therefore, the evidence strongly supports that local anesthesia is needed.", "answer_choice": "A"}
